config.title: Vulnerability and adaptation to climate-related hazards
config.abstract: Extreme weather events, such as floods, droughts, tornadoes, and heatwaves  occur more often and are stronger due to climate change and impact more than half the world’s population. A third of those affected are also highly vulnerable and require adaptation to a changing climate.

content.text: Intro
Chapter will focus on targets 1 and 2:
13.1 Strengthen resilience and adaptive capacity to climate-related hazards and natural disasters in all countries
13.2 Integrate climate change measures into national policies, strategies and planning.
Global mean temperature
Several indicators provide evidence of a changing climate. Globally, temperatures have been steadily increasing compared to the average for the period of 1850-1900.
Number of hot days

content.title: Top 50 countries with most hot days in 2020s
content.subtitle: Number of hot days per year, 2020 average

scenes.text: As the average temperature increases, more extreme weather events happen more frequently. This includes ‘hot days’ defined as days with temperature in excess of XX degrees.

While you might expect these countries to be on a top-50 list by the number of hot days, the number of days has increased quite dramatically in these countries compared to the average in the period YYYY-ZZZZ.

content.text: Precipitation

The impacts of a changing climate not only affects average and extreme temperatures. Changing precipitation patterns are also affected, and irregular precipitation, droughts and floods are impacting agricultural production possibilities as well as the health and safety of individuals.
[GRAPHICS]
Sea level rise

content.title: Global mean sea level change
content.subtitle: Millimeters
content.visdescription: As temperatures increase, sea levels are increasing as well. Since the early 1990s, sea levels have increased by approximately 100 millimeters. And they are expected to rise by XX millimeters by [2050/2100].

content.text: Effects of climate change
Effects of Climate Change on People, Communities, and Infrastructure
Highlight key climate-induced disasters (floods, droughts, storms, heatwaves).
Provide examples of recent disasters impacting human lives and infrastructure.
Emphasize increasing frequency and severity.
People at high-risk of climate impact
1.2 billion people are at high risk of climate impact. Based on exposure (4 climate hazards) and vulnerability.
Exposure to climate hazards
Climate change

Visualize the following 4 climate hazards using the following data on a global map and introduce the new World Bank Corporate Scorecard Indicator: "People at High Risk to Climate-related Hazards." Starting by presenting population density, hazard exposure and vulnerability at a global scale one by one, and finally overlaying to show the ‘population at risk’ at a global scale.

content.title: Climate related disasters (floods, droughts, cyclones, and heatwaves)
content.subtitle: Vis subtitle

scenes.text: There are four categories of climate-related disasters that the World Bank analyses in terms of exposure to climate hazards

Vulnerability
Being exposed to climate hazards does not tell the full story of climate hazards. Whether people are affected by these events depends on their level of vulnerability.
[Use previous visualization showing global population but this time showing the share being vulnerable]
How do we determine who is vulnerable?
[scrollie of tile map showing the three categories of vulnerability
poverty/education
Social  (social protection, financial inclusion)
Infrastructure/access (water, energy, access)
People at risk
[Map of 100/200 people representing global population overlapping exposure and vulnerability - overlapping the maps above]
[Interactive tile map
Combining the two dimensions of exposure and vulnerability to show people at risk
Allow users to choose individual countries
On the card that pops up for each country, present a drop down menu choosing dimensions of exposure and vulnerability
Zoom-in on country case studies.

Select two countries with similar hazard exposure, but differing vulnerability and thus differing numbers of people at risk. [SUGGESTIONS: Jamaica vs. Dominican Republic. Thailand vs. Philippines. For extreme differences, look at Korea vs. Lao to visualize differences between a high-income and lower-middle income country from the same region.]
Explain differences in adaptive capacities and implications for risk.
Drill-down into subnational level
Highlight spatial variation within a single country
Showcase regional disparities in exposure, vulnerability and risk.
Increasing Climate Risks
Present data illustrating changes in climate risk between 2010 and 2021 from key indicator.
Contrast two countries with different rates of progress:
Country showing significant progress in reducing climate risk.
Country with minimal or negative progress.
Discuss factors influencing these trends (e.g., policy implementation, investment in infrastructure, institutional capacity).
Building resilience to reduce vulnerability
What can governments do to improve climate resilience? Introduce the Adaptation and Resilience Readiness Assessment Tool:
Brief explanation of the tool’s purpose and approach.
Highlight the tool's relevance in prioritizing resilience actions.

Apply this tool to the two previously selected countries [Present interactive graphics that peel back layers of the country-level readiness scores, illustrating their subscores and indicators, with explanations of why interesting.]
Demonstrate how these detailed readiness indicators directly correlate with observed differences in vulnerability, or not.
Provide practical examples of actions prioritized or implemented, explicitly tied to specific readiness indicators and scores.
